title: Clean Up the Equation
learningTarget: I can combine like terms and use the distributive property to solve multi-step equations.
whyItMatters: This lesson builds on inverse operations. Multi-step equations usually need cleanup first: distribute through parentheses, combine like terms, then undo the remaining operations in a balanced way.
realWorldApplication: On CAASPP, multi-step equations often appear inside word problems or student-work examples. In real life, the same skill helps you work backward through bundle prices, fees, subscriptions, sports events, and budgets.

standards.label: Explain why each equation-solving step is valid.
Solve linear equations in one variable.
Create equations from real situations and use them to solve problems.

overviewPrompt:
Create a short overview video for 11th grade students preparing for the CAASPP math test.

Lesson title: Clean Up the Equation

Main idea: Some equations need to be cleaned up before you solve them. First use the distributive property, then combine like terms, then use inverse operations to isolate x.

Use this exact example:

Solve 3(x + 8) + 2x = 79.

Step 1: Distribute the 3.
3(x + 8) becomes 3x + 24, so the equation becomes:
3x + 24 + 2x = 79

Step 2: Combine like terms.
3x + 2x = 5x, so the equation becomes:
5x + 24 = 79

Step 3: Undo the +24.
Subtract 24 from both sides:
5x = 55

Step 4: Undo multiplication by 5.
Divide both sides by 5:
x = 11

Check:
3(11 + 8) + 2(11) = 79
3(19) + 22 = 79
57 + 22 = 79
79 = 79

The final answer is x = 11.

Keep the video short, clear, and student-friendly. Emphasize that multi-step equations are not harder because of one big trick. They are solved by doing small legal moves in the right order: distribute, combine like terms, undo, and check.

vocabulary.definition: Terms with the same variable part, like 3x and 2x. They can be combined.
Multiplying a number by each term inside parentheses. Example: 3(x + 8) becomes 3x + 24.
Make an expression cleaner by combining like terms or removing parentheses.
An equation that takes more than one math move to solve.
Looking at someone else's work to find the exact step where the math stopped being valid.

sections.title: Overview Reflection
sections.studentText: After watching the overview, explain the main point in your own words.

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain why a multi-step equation often needs to be simplified before you solve it.
questions.feedback: A strong response explains that simplifying clears away parentheses and like terms so the inverse operations are easier and valid.

sections.title: Quick Start
sections.studentText: Before solving a full equation, check whether you can spot the cleanup move.

questions.feedback: Distribute the 3 to both terms: 3 times x is 3x, and 3 times 8 is 24.

sections.title: Watch: Solving equations with the distributive property
sections.watchFor: Watch for the order of the moves: distribute first, combine like terms if possible, then use inverse operations to isolate the variable.

contextQuestions.prompt: When an equation has parentheses like 3(x + 8), what cleanup move usually comes first?
contextQuestions.feedback: Parentheses often need the distributive property before the equation is ready for inverse operations.

contextQuestions.prompt: What does it mean to combine like terms before solving?
contextQuestions.feedback: A strong answer says you combine terms with the same variable part, such as 3x + 2x becoming 5x.

contextQuestions.prompt: Which pair can be combined as like terms?
contextQuestions.feedback: 4x and -2x both have the same variable part, x.

contextQuestions.prompt: Why is it useful to check a multi-step equation solution after you solve?
commonMisconceptions: checking is only for one-step equations
checking means redoing the same work
contextQuestions.feedback: A strong answer says checking catches mistakes by substituting the answer into the original equation.

sections.title: Clean Up Before You Undo
sections.studentText: A multi-step equation is easier when you separate the cleanup moves from the undo moves.

workedExample.commonMistake: A common mistake is writing 3(x + 8) as 3x + 8. The 3 must multiply both x and 8.

sections.title: Hands-On: Choose the Next Legal Move
sections.studentText: Use the undo board to solve 3(x + 8) + 2x = 79. Pick the next algebra move from the bank and drag it into the box. The board only moves forward when the choice keeps the math valid.

states.feedback: Start with the parentheses. What has to happen to 3(x + 8)?
states.wrongFeedback: Not yet. The expression still has parentheses, so clean those up first.

states.feedback: Good. Distributing changed 3(x + 8) into 3x + 24. Now look for like terms.
states.wrongFeedback: Not yet. Before undoing the +24, combine the x terms on the left.
Not yet. The variable term has +24 attached, so undo the addition first.

states.feedback: Good. Subtracting 24 from both sides leaves 5x = 55. What undoes multiplying by 5?
states.wrongFeedback: Not yet. x is being multiplied by 5, so use the inverse of multiplication.

states.feedback: Solved: x = 11. You distributed, combined like terms, subtracted 24, then divided by 5.

sections.title: Real-World Equation: 3-on-3 Tournament Bundles
sections.studentText: A student group is running a lunchtime 3-on-3 basketball tournament. They sell 3 team bundles. Each bundle includes one entry wristband that costs x dollars plus an $8 snack card. They also sell 2 extra wristbands for x dollars each. The total collected is $79. The situation can be modeled by 3(x + 8) + 2x = 79.

image.alt: High school students at a basketball tournament sign-up table with wristbands, snack cards, and a clipboard.
image.caption: The image gives the tournament setting. Use the page text for the exact numbers and equation.

questions.prompt: In the tournament equation 3(x + 8) + 2x = 79, what does x represent?
questions.feedback: x represents the price of one entry wristband.
Distribute to get 3x + 24 + 2x = 79, combine to get 5x + 24 = 79, subtract 24, then divide by 5. x = 11.

questions.prompt: Explain what x = 11 means in the tournament situation.
questions.feedback: x = 11 means each entry wristband costs $11.

questions.prompt: A student solves 4(x + 3) = 44 by writing 4x + 3 = 44. What is the mistake?

options.text: They forgot to divide by 4 first.
They did not distribute the 4 to the 3.
They should have added 3 to both sides.

questions.feedback: 4(x + 3) should become 4x + 12 because 4 multiplies both terms inside the parentheses.

questions.prompt: Explain the first two cleanup moves you would use to solve 5x + 2(x - 4) = 34.
questions.feedback: First distribute the 2 to get 5x + 2x - 8 = 34. Then combine like terms to get 7x - 8 = 34.
Distribute: 5x + 2x - 8 = 34. Combine: 7x - 8 = 34. Add 8, then divide by 7. x = 6.

questions.prompt: A student writes 2(x + 5) + 3x = 2x + 5 + 3x. What should the corrected expression be?
Explain how to tell whether a student's multi-step equation solution has a distribution mistake.
questions.feedback: A strong answer says to check whether the outside number multiplied every term inside the parentheses, including constants and signs.

gate.completion: Student attempts the overview reflection, video questions, real-world task, practice, and exit check.
gate.mastery: At least 70% on the exit check, with practice available for revision until the cleanup moves make sense.
gate.remediation: Review distribution and combine-like-terms moves, then retry two multi-step equations with new numbers.
gate.enrichment: Create a believable wrong solution with a distribution or like-terms error, then explain exactly how to fix it.
